experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I have no concrete role information yet; as a good Servant on round 1, a 2-person team including myself is reasonable. Approving an initial team helps generate information from quest outcome, and since I am good, the team has at least one good member. My vote reveals willingness to test an early standard team rather than stall.
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil, so 0 and 3 are the only players not identified as evil besides me. On quest 1 with a 2-person team, [0,3] excludes known evil and is very likely good. As good, I should approve a clean team, and approving does not strongly expose me because rejecting such a team would be suspicious.
Player 1: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0, 2, and 3 are good. The proposed team [0,3] contains no evil players, so if approved it will almost certainly pass and give Good an early success. As evil in the team voting phase, optimal play is to reject clean all-good teams when possible, especially on round 1 where there is little penalty for rejection. My reject vote gives little concrete information because rejecting an opening team is common, and it helps prevent a guaranteed good quest result.
Player 0: No concrete role info yet from quest outcomes or voting patterns. As a Good Servant on round 1 quest 1, a standard heuristic is to approve an initial 2-person team including myself unless there is strong suspicion, which there is not. Approving preserves information flow and avoids needless early rejects. My vote reveals mild trust/neutrality toward player 3, which is acceptable given no evidence yet.
Player 4: I know player 1 is my evil teammate; players 0, 2, and 3 are nominally good from my perspective, with no behavioral info yet. In round 1 on a 2-player quest, a team of two non-evil players [0,3] excludes both evil players, which is bad for evil because it can score an easy success and build trust between good players. As Minion, optimal play is usually to reject teams without any evil presence when possible, while keeping my vote explainable. My reject reveals slight resistance to a clean-looking team, but early votes are noisy and this helps prevent a good-only success if others also reject.

Quest 1 succeeded on team [0,3], so at least one of 0/3 is likely Good, possibly both; no evil info from that quest unless a teammate chose to pass, but neither evil teammate was on it. As leader on quest 2 (size 3), optimal evil play is to include at least one evil, ideally exactly one evil to keep suspicion manageable while giving us a fail. Including both 1 and 4 risks obvious blame if the quest fails. Best team is one evil plus two from the successful prior team to maximize approval chances and plausibility. Proposing [0,3,4] lets me fail the quest and looks natural because it keeps the successful pair and adds myself as leader. This reveals little beyond normal leader behavior and helps Evil by likely securing an approved sabotagable team.

Player 2: I am Merlin and know player 4 is Evil. The proposed team [0,3,4] includes a known Evil player, so as Good I should reject it. Rejecting also avoids legitimizing 4 and does not reveal more than necessary, since any reasonable Good player can oppose a team containing a suspicious member after a clean prior quest from [0,3].
Player 3: I know the previous successful team was [0,3], which gives weak positive evidence that 0 and I are good since no fail occurred, though one evil could have passed. As a Servant on team voting, optimal play is to approve teams that preserve successful members and avoid unnecessary exclusions. The proposed team [0,3,4] keeps both members of the successful quest and adds one new player, which is a standard good follow-up on quest 2. Approving reveals consistency with trusting a proven-success core and does not meaningfully hurt the good team.
Player 4: I know player 1 is evil with me; team [0,3] previously succeeded, so at least one of 0 or 3 is likely good, but both could also be good. Current proposed team [0,3,4] includes me, an evil player, giving evil the option to fail quest 2. As Minion, I want teams with evil on them approved, especially when they can swing the game. Approving reveals little beyond normal voting and helps evil get onto mission 2.
Player 0: Player 3 led the successful first quest with team [0,3], so both 0 (me) and 3 look somewhat good, though a single success doesn't clear either completely. Player 4 now adds themselves to that successful core, which is a reasonable progression and not inherently suspicious. As a good Servant in team voting, I should generally approve teams that keep successful members and only add one unknown, especially early on. Approving preserves momentum and gives useful info from quest 2; rejecting this team would be too conservative with limited evidence.
Player 1: Player 4 is my evil teammate, and the proposed team [0,3,4] includes exactly one evil. Quest 1 succeeded with [0,3], so at least one of 0/3 is good, likely both from table perspective. As evil on team 2, having teammate 4 on the mission gives us sabotage potential, but approving a team with only one known evil can create ambiguity if it fails and also advances an evil-controlled mission slot onto the quest. Since this is just a vote and not obviously suspicious for me to support a 3-person team containing 4 after a successful [0,3], optimal evil play is to approve and hope 4 can choose the best sabotage timing. My vote reveals little because mixed votes are common early.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are Evil. Team [0,3] contains no known Evil from my perspective, and this exact pair previously succeeded with 0 fails. Rejecting a clean-looking team gives Evil room to push worse teams and may expose my knowledge pattern. Approving is optimal for Good and consistent with available evidence.
Player 0: I know Quest 1 with team [0,3] succeeded, while Quest 2 with [0,3,4] had exactly one fail. That strongly suggests player 4 is evil, though it is still possible one of 0 or 3 caused the fail and chose to pass on Quest 1. As a good Servant, I should favor teams that minimize inclusion of the most suspicious player. The current phase is team voting on [0,3], which is the same successful pair from Quest 1 and excludes the standout suspect 4. Approving keeps pressure off good info and supports a likely-good team; rejecting gives little benefit and risks worse teams later. My vote reveals trust in 0 and 3 relative to 4, which is consistent with the available evidence and helps good coordinate.
Player 1: I know 4 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,3,4] had exactly 1 fail, which could be 4 or me if I were on it; since I wasn't, suspicion should be on 4, 0, or 3, but I know 4 is evil and 0/3 are good. Current proposed team [0,3] excludes both evils, so it is a strong good team likely to succeed and give Good a second point. As evil, I should reject good-only teams whenever plausible. My vote reveals little because rejecting a successful-looking team is somewhat suspicious, but preventing or delaying a clean good quest is worth it, and we still have my teammate likely under some suspicion from prior fail.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. From quest history, team [0,3] succeeded on Quest 1, so 0 and 3 are somewhat good-leaning. Team [0,3,4] failed with exactly one fail, which strongly implicates 4, since adding 4 to a previously successful pair caused the fail. As a good player on [0,3], approving the repeat team maximizes chance of success and also tests the strongest available good-leaning pair. Voting reject would risk moving to less reliable teams. My vote reveals I trust the evidence-based team, which helps Good.
Player 4: I know player 1 is evil with me; players 0 and 3 were on the successful Quest 1 team [0,3], so at least one of them is likely good. Quest 2 team [0,3,4] failed with exactly one fail, which could only have come from me because 1 was not on the team, so from others' perspective suspicion should point strongly at me or possibly create doubt between 0 and 3. Now the proposed team is [0,3] again for Quest 3. As evil in team voting, I want to prevent likely-good teams from going on quests, especially a 2-player team that already succeeded once and excludes both evil players. Rejecting is optimal to avoid handing good another likely success and to push for a more manipulable team later. My reject also aligns with plausible self-preservation since I was on the failed team and may want a different composition; it doesn't reveal more than necessary.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are Evil. The proposed team [0,1,3] includes known Evil player 1, so from Good's perspective this team is highly dangerous, especially on quest 4 where a fail would tie the game. Rejecting avoids sending a known evil on the mission. My vote may reveal some resistance to 1, but preserving mission success matters more than concealment here.
Player 4: I know 1 is evil with me. Team [0,1,3] includes exactly one known evil, which is good for us because on quest 4 a fail would put Evil to 2 points. From public info, 0 and 3 were on two successful 2-person quests, while my team with 0,3,4 failed due to me, so 0 and 3 likely look good to others and this team is very likely to pass votes. As evil, I should approve a team containing my teammate that can fail the quest. Approving reveals little since many players would also approve a plausible team.
Player 3: I know 0 and I were on two successful quests together, while the only failed quest was [0,3,4], which strongly implicates 4 as evil and makes 0 look good. Player 1 is less confirmed, but excluding 4 is optimal. As a good servant in team voting on quest 4, approving [0,1,3] is reasonable because it keeps the strong core of 0 and 3 and avoids the most suspicious player 4. My vote reveals I support a team consistent with available evidence, which helps good more than rejecting an actually plausible team.
Player 1: I know 4 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,3,4] had exactly one fail, which could be from 4 or me if present; since I wasn't on it, that points to 4 failing or possibly a hidden evil if mission rules allowed, so 0/3 are somewhat cleaner publicly. Current phase is team voting on [0,1,3]. As evil Assassin on quest 4, I want a team with me on it approved so I can potentially fail and bring evil to 2 points. Voting approve is optimal. My vote supporting my own team is normal and doesn't reveal much; rejecting a team containing myself would look suspicious and hurt evil chances.
Player 0: Known info: team [0,3] succeeded twice with 0 fails, strongly suggesting 0 and 3 are good. Team [0,3,4] had exactly 1 fail, so 4 is the most likely evil among {0,3,4}, making 0 and 3 even cleaner. Player 1 is less cleared but this proposed team avoids 4, the strongest suspect. On quest 4 at 2-1, good should prioritize a team built around the strongest clears; [0,1,3] is reasonable and likely optimal among available options. My vote reveals trust in the cleared core and willingness to test 1; rejecting would delay without better evidence. Approving helps good more than it helps evil here.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are Evil. The proposed team [0,2,3] excludes both known evil players, so from my information it is an all-good team and should succeed. In team voting on quest 5, approving the strongest good-only team is optimal. My approve vote signals support for a clean team, which helps Good more than rejecting and risking worse proposals or auto-pass dynamics.
Player 4: I know player 1 is my evil teammate, and I am evil. The proposed team [0,2,3] contains no known evil players, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and hand Good the game on quest 5. In team voting on the final quest, optimal evil play is to reject any team that appears all-good, especially on round 1 when there are more chances to force a different team. My reject also subtly signals opposition without revealing more than necessary; approving would strongly help Good.
Player 3: As a Good Servant, I know only from quest history. Teams with 0 and 3 succeeded on quest 2, so 0 and I are likely good. Failures were on [0,3,4] and [0,1,3], each with exactly one fail, strongly implicating 4 and 1 as evil if 0 and I are good. That leaves 2 as the likely remaining good player. Final quest team [0,2,3] excludes the two strongest evil suspects and includes the likely three goods, so approving maximizes good's chance. My vote supports a team that reveals little extra but is the best available composition.
Player 0: I am Good Servant. Known data: Team [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 are likely good. Team [0,3,4] failed, which strongly implicates 4 as evil since 0 and 3 looked clean. Team [0,1,3] failed, which then implicates 1 as evil. That leaves team [0,2,3] as the best possible final quest team, with 2 the remaining likely good player. On team voting for quest 5, approving this team maximizes Good's chance to win and is consistent with prior evidence. My approve vote also signals confidence in 0/3 and suspicion on 1/4, which helps Good more than Evil.
Player 1: I know player 4 is my evil teammate. From quest history, teams [0,3,4] and [0,1,3] each produced exactly one fail, while [0,3] succeeded. That strongly implicates 4 for the first fail and me for the third fail, making 0 and 3 likely good; 2 is less tested but likely good enough. The current phase is team voting on the final quest, which needs 3 players and only one fail to lose for Good. This proposed team [0,2,3] contains no known evil, so if approved it likely succeeds and Good wins 3-2. As evil, optimal play is to reject any all-good team and hope for a later proposal including me or 4. My vote reveals limited info since evil should oppose this team anyway, and one reject on a likely good team is acceptable.
